steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls between the noun "song" and the quoted title “Oh What a Day” by the Dells. The question asks which punctuation — if any — belongs between a noun and an immediately following quotation-mark title.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'song:': A colon introduces a list or explanation that follows as an independent element. A quoted song title is not a separate clause or list; it is directly attached to the noun it names. ✗
  • Choice B — 'song,': A comma would mark the title as a nonessential interruption, implying the sentence is complete without it. The title is essential — it identifies which song. ✗
  • Choice C — 'song;': A semicolon joins two independent clauses. "Oh What a Day" by the Dells is not an independent clause, so a semicolon is grammatically impossible here. ✗
  • Choice D — 'song': A noun is written directly before its quotation-mark title with no intervening punctuation. "The hit song “Oh What a Day”" is a single noun phrase. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
